title: Lesson 383
transcript: We're now gonna go through a number of different tools, technologies that can be used to help protect data. Let's start with DRM, digital rights management. At the most basic level, what DRM is, is you are encrypting some data. And then by encrypting that data, it allows you to then control who can do what with that data. Who can decrypt it and look at it? Who can modify the data? Who can copy it? Who could print it? Who could run it on? So where you may have come across DRM technologies is old DVDs. If you remember DVDs, right? We used to watch movies off of laser discs. They're off of little like disc things. DVDs were often region restricted. So if you bought a DVD from Japan, it wouldn't work on your DVD player in Canada or the US. This is DRM technology. It's encrypting data. And then because that data is encrypted, you can control how that data is used. And you can apply this to an individual file, to an entire DVD, et cetera. And you can apply it. Another perfect use case of this is if you download music from the Apple Music Store onto your iphone there's drm in place there that or there has been in the past to make sure that you can't then copy that music file onto another device so like i said the most basic level drm is the idea of encrypting data And then because you've encrypted the data, you can now control how that data is used, who can use it for what purposes, who can edit the data, who can print the data, play it in certain places, copy it, etc. So that's the whole idea of digital rights management, encrypting data, which allows you to control who can do what with the data. IRM, information rights management, is a subset of DRM. And IRM tools are typically used in organizations to control documents. That's why it's referred to as information rights management. So a perfect example of this is PDF documents. You can control who can do what. Like if you have Adobe, the full version of Adobe Acrobat, you can actually put a bunch of controls in place for a PDF document. You can make it so no one can edit it. You can make it so no one can print it. You can make it so no one can... you know, copy, like make some, I don't know, I've already given you examples. You can put a bunch of controls in place. I ran out of examples there. You can put a bunch of controls in place on a PDF. This is a perfect example of information rights management. So just IRM is a substantive DRM typically used in organizations for documents like PDFs. DLP, Data Loss Prevention Tools. Fundamentally, what DLP systems are all about is protecting sensitive data. And let's spend just a second on here. This is important. DLP data loss prevention tools are about preventing the unauthorized usage, transfer, copying, et cetera, of sensitive data. And the key word here being sensitive. Because DLP tools can't prevent every document from being emailed, or every document from being saved, or every bit of data from being copied from here to there, right? If you just prevented everything from moving across the network from being stored, you know, you wouldn't be able to do anything anymore. To have any sort of effective DLP system, the first thing you need to do is you need to know what is your sensitive data. which implies you need data classification. You need to know what is your sensitive data. And the way you know that is you've classified the data. So this is an extremely important requirement of having an effective DLP solution. You first need to know what is your sensitive data. Is it credit card numbers? Is it social insurance numbers? Is it some sort of intellectual property? Is it certain images or graphics that are being produced by an advertising company? You need to know what your sensitive data is so that you can then have DLP tools that will control what is done with that sensitive data. DLP tools typically provide kind of three major capabilities. The first is they will help with classification, with helping you identify what is or isn't sensitive data. So, for example, it's typically very easy to recognize a credit card number because it's usually 16 digits. There's a check digit there. So it's very easy to look at a 16 digit number and say, hey, is this a valid credit card number? And so your DLP system can essentially help you classify what is a credit card number just by knowing what the structure of a credit card number is or a social insurance number. So DLP systems can be used to help you identify what is your sensitive data. They can then be used to control what is done with that data. Is it allowed to be, like let's say on an endpoint device, is someone allowed to email certain sensitive data? Are they allowed to copy data out of a database into an Excel sheet? Are they allowed to transfer certain data onto a USB stick or store it on a hard drive somewhere? So this is often referred to as data in use. So DLP systems can control how data is used. And typically, the way the DLP systems will control the data in use is through endpoint devices by having a monitoring agent on an endpoint device, like a laptop or something like that. Another major use case for DLP systems is data in motion. So this is data that's moving across the network. So for the DLP system to be able to look at what data is transiting the network, you're going to have to have DLP sensors spread across your network segments. So the DLP agent can then see what data is being sent from system A to system B or egressing out of the network. So this is another sort of major category of DLP systems where they'll actually be looking at data in motion. And then the final major category of DLP systems is data at rest. So this is data that's being stored in a file server, in a database somewhere, et cetera. There are DLP systems that can look at data in rest. DLP agents, high level, right? They're helping you identify what is sensitive data. They're looking to see if there's unauthorized usage of that sensitive data. And if there is, they can perform some sort of enforcement action, like prevent the data from being emailed, prevent the data from being copied to a USB stack, prevent the data from, stop the data from leaving the network. et cetera, so they can perform enforcement actions. And then they can do this across these sort of three major use cases of data, data in use on an endpoint device, data in motion across the network and data at rest on a hard drive or storage server or database somewhere. So that's super high level. That's DLP technologies and how they can be used to help protect sensitive data in an organization. And then the final one I'll mention here, protection tools, is CASBs, Cloud Access Service Brokers. I'll talk about the details in a second, but let's just first talk high-level, like what are CASBs? So of course, you know what the cloud is. There's all these cloud services, software as a service, things like that. So let's say a company has decided to use Box as a data storage service. So you've got now two entities, right? You've got the company and their employees here, and then you've got Box over here, which is the cloud service provider. Where a CASB would sit is in between the two. So the CASB is almost like, if you're familiar with proxies, a CASB is essentially a proxy. A proxy means that all the traffic going between, say, the customer and the cloud service provider has to pass through the proxy through the cloud service broker. Cloud Access Service Broker. So the CASB essentially acts as a system in the middle, as a proxy, such that all the communications between the consumer and the cloud service provider back and forth pass through the CASB. Why? So the CASB can make decisions. So for example, a perfect example of this is a CASB solution could have, say, a DLP capability in it. So if a customer, sorry, if an employee went to store some file full of credit card numbers in it, the CASB solution could look at that file, say, oh, like there's a bunch of sensitive data in this. And they just wouldn't the CASB would not allow that data to pass on to the cloud service provider to box that should not have a copy of that data. So because the CASB is acting as an intermediary between the consumer and the cloud service provider, it allows the CASB to make decisions as to what it's going to permit or deny. And so this is why when you see statements here like policy enforcement, the CASB can make sure that, you know, what data is being sent to the cloud service broker is appropriate. Or another really important thing CASBs will do is they'll be used to try and eliminate shadow IT. Shadow IT is where consumers go in, like employees at a company, go and use cloud services that they're not authorized to access, like Google Drive or Box or something. If there's a CASB sitting between employees and all of the cloud services, the CASB can control which cloud services the employee is allowed to access. And if they try to go and access something they're not supposed to, the CASB can block that. And so obviously your policies would say what is or is not permitted, and the CASB would be making decisions then as to what it's gonna allow through or not. And these CASBs can have all sorts of different threat protection capabilities built into them, things like DRM, or sorry, like DLP and stuff like that. So that's the idea of CASBs. Basically, just think of them as an intermediary between the consumer and the cloud service providers. And the CASB is making decisions as to what it's going to prevent or deny to help you enforce your security controls.
